name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ إِحْدَى ٱلطَّآئِفَتَيْنِ أَنَّهَا لَكُمْ وَتَوَدُّونَ أَنَّ غَيْرَ ذَاتِ ٱلشَّوْكَةِ تَكُونُ لَكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُحِقَّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعَ دَابِرَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧)
```

```english
When Allah promised you [victory over] one of the two companies, [saying], ‘It is for you,’ you were eager that it should be the one that was unarmed. But Allah desires to confirm the truth with His words, and to root out the faithless, (7)
```

```arabic
لِيُحِقَّ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٨)
```

```english
so that He may confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse. (8)
```

```arabic
إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّى مُمِدُّكُم بِأَلْفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ (٩)
```

```english
When you appealed to your Lord for help, He answered you: ‘I will aid you with a thousand angels in a file.’ (9)
```

```arabic
وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ بِهِۦ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠)
```

```english
Allah did not appoint it but as a good news, and to reassure your hearts. Victory comes only from Allah. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (10)
```

```arabic
إِذْ يُغَشِّيكُمُ ٱلنُّعَاسَ أَمَنَةً مِّنْهُ وَيُنَزِّلُ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً لِّيُطَهِّرَكُم بِهِۦ وَيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمْ رِجْزَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيَرْبِطَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتَ بِهِ ٱلْأَقْدَامَ (١١)
```

```english
When He covered you with a trance as a [sense of] security from Him, and He sent down water from the sky to purify you with it, and to repel from you the defilement of Satan, and to fortify your hearts, and to make [your] feet steady with it. (11)
```

```arabic
إِذْ يُوحِى رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ أَنِّى مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۚ سَأُلْقِى فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلرُّعْبَ فَٱضْرِبُوا۟ فَوْقَ ٱلْأَعْنَاقِ وَٱضْرِبُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ كُلَّ بَنَانٍ (١٢)
```

```english
Then your Lord signaled to the angels: ‘I am indeed with you; so steady the faithful. I will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless. So strike their necks, and strike their every limb joint!’ (12)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ وَمَن يُشَاقِقِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١٣)
```

```english
That, because they defied Allah and His Apostle. And whoever defies Allah and His Apostle, Allah is indeed severe in retribution. (13)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكُمْ فَذُوقُوهُ وَأَنَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٤)
```

```english
‘Taste this, and [know] that for the faithless is the punishment of the Fire.’ (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses refer to the Battle of Badr, which was the first military expedition (الغزوة) in Islam. The apparent context of the verses suggests they were revealed after its conclusion, as will become clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللهُ إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ أَنَّها لَكُمْ وَتَوَدُّونَ أَنَّ غَيْرَ ذاتِ الشَّوْكَةِ تَكُونُ لَكُمْ وَيُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ وَيَقْطَعَ دابِرَ الْكافِرِينَ*» **And when Allah promised you one of the two parties [at Badr] that it would be yours, and you wished that the one without stings should be yours, and Allah desired to confirm the truth by His words and to uproot the faithless** (8:7). That is: "And remember when Allah promised you." This is an exposition of Allah’s favors and an enumeration of His blessings upon them, so they might have insight (بصيرة) that Allah, the Glorified, does not confront them with any matter nor bring them any judgment except with the truth (الحق). Within it lies the preservation of their interests and the prosperity of their fortune, so they should not differ among themselves, nor dislike what He chooses for them, but rather entrust their affairs to Him and obey Him and His Messenger.

What is meant by "the two parties" (الطائفتين) are the caravan (العير) and the army (النفير). The caravan (العير) was the trade convoy of Quraysh containing their merchandise and wealth, guarded by forty men including Abu Sufyan ibn Harb. The army (النفير) was the military force of Quraysh, numbering approximately one thousand men.

His saying: «*إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ*» (one of the two parties) is the second object of His saying: «*يَعِدُكُمُ*» (He promised you). His saying: «*أَنَّها لَكُمْ*» (that it would be yours) is a substitute (بدل) for it. His saying: «*وَتَوَدُّونَ*» (and you wished) is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال). What is meant by "the one without stings" (غير ذات الشوكة) is the party without military might, which was the caravan, as it had fewer men and less equipment than the army. **The sting** (الشوكة) refers to sharpness or military prowess, a metaphor (استعارة) derived from thorns.

His saying: «*وَيُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ*» (and Allah desired to confirm the truth by His words) is also in the position of a circumstantial clause. Confirming the truth (إحقاق الحق) means making it manifest and establishing it by arranging its effects upon it. The "words of Allah" (كلمات الله) are what He has decreed regarding the victory of His prophets and the manifestation of His true religion. The Exalted said: «*وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ*» **Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will be the victors** (37:171-173). And the Exalted said: «*يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَاللهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ*» **They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah shall perfect His light though the faithless should be averse. It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse** (61:8-9). It has also been recited as "by His word" (بكلمته) in the singular, which is more appropriate and closer in meaning. **The root** (الدابر) refers to that which follows a thing, being connected and attached to it. "Cutting off the root" (قطع دابر الشيء) is a metaphor for its total annihilation and uprooting (استئصاله) such that nothing remains of its subsequent effects or related branches.

The meaning of the verse is: Remember when Allah promised you that one of the two parties would be yours—that you would prevail over it by Allah’s help—either the caravan or the army. You wished that the party would be the caravan because of what you knew of the military might (شوكة) of the army, their strength, and their intensity, coupled with your own weakness and lowliness. Yet the situation was that Allah willed the opposite: that you should encounter the army so He might grant you victory over them, manifest the truth He had decreed to appear, uproot the disbelievers, and cut off their last remnant.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ*» **That He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse** (8:8). The apparent context is that the "Lām" (لـ) denotes purpose (للغاية). His saying: «*لِيُحِقَ*» (That He might confirm) is connected to His saying: «*يَعِدُكُمُ اللهُ*» (Allah promised you). That is, Allah only promised you that—and He does not break His promise—so that He might thereby confirm the truth and nullify falsehood, even if the guilty (المجرمون) should dislike and reject it.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: «*لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَ*» (That He might confirm the truth) is not a mere repetition of His saying: «*وَيُرِيدُ اللهُ أَنْ يُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ*» (and Allah desired to confirm the truth by His words), even if it shares the same meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ فَاسْتَجابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّي مُمِدُّكُمْ بِأَلْفٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ*» **When you appealed to your Lord for help, He answered you: ‘I will aid you with a thousand angels in succession’** (8:9). **Appealing for help** (الاستغاثة) is the request for aid (الغوث), which is assistance (النصرة), as in His saying: «*فَاسْتَغاثَهُ الَّذِي مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ عَلَى الَّذِي مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ*» **whereupon he who was of his party sought his help against him who was of his enemies** (28:15). "Aiding" (الإمداد) is well-known. His saying: «*مُرْدِفِينَ*» (in succession) is from **succession** (الإرداف), which is when a rider makes another person sit behind him (ردفاً). The "ridf" (الردف) is the follower. Al-Raghib said: **The ridf** (الردف) is the follower; the *ridf* of a woman is her posterior; **succession** (الترادف) is following in sequence; the *rādif* is the one who lags behind; and the *murdif* is the one in front who has placed another behind him. [End of quote].

With this meaning, the verse aligns with what Allah says while referring to this story in Surah Al-Imran: «*وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنْتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَنْ يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَنْ يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِثَلاثَةِ آلافٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُنْزَلِينَ بَلى إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِخَمْسَةِ آلافٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ وَما جَعَلَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا بُشْرى لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُمْ بِهِ وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ*» **Certainly Allah helped you at Badr, when you were abject [in the enemy’s eyes]. So be wary of Allah, so that you may give thanks. When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you that your Lord should aid you with three thousand angels sent down? Yes, if you are steadfast and God-conscious, and they come at you in this heat of theirs, your Lord will aid you with five thousand marked angels.’ Allah did not appoint it but as a good news for you, and to reassure your hearts with it; and victory comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise** (3:123-126).

Comparing the verses from the two Surahs clarifies that the meaning of sending down a thousand angels "in succession" (مردفين) is the descent of a thousand who are followed by others. Thus, the thousand in succession corresponds to the three thousand sent down.

This exposes the invalidity of the claim that "in succession" (مردفين) means that each of the thousand was followed by another thousand, making them two thousand. Similarly invalid is the claim that it means some followed after others, or that they came behind the Muslims (interpreting *murdifīn* as *rādifīn*), or that they were behind the Muslims in the sense of being in front of the Muslim army to cast terror into the hearts of the disbelievers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَما جَعَلَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا بُشْرى وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ بِهِ قُلُوبُكُمْ وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ*» **Allah did not appoint it but as a good news, and to reassure your hearts with it; and victory comes only from Allah. Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise** (8:10). The two pronouns in His saying: «*جَعَلَهُ*» (He appointed it) and «*بِهِ*» (with it) refer to the aiding with angels, as indicated by the context. The meaning is that the aiding with angels was only for the purpose of good news and the reassurance of your souls, not so that the disbelievers would be destroyed by their hands, as indicated by His saying later: «*إِذْ يُوحِي رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلائِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَأُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ*» **When your Lord revealed to the angels: ‘I am with you, so steady the faithful. I will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless’** (8:12).

This supports what some have mentioned: that the angels did not descend to kill the polytheists, nor did they kill any of them. One-third or one-half of those killed were slain by Ali (peace be upon him), and the remaining two-thirds or half by the rest of the Muslims. The role of the angels was to increase the perceived numbers of the Muslims when they mingled with the enemy, to steady the hearts of the Muslims, and to cast terror into the hearts of the polytheists. More will be said on this later.

His saying: «*وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ*» (and victory comes only from Allah. Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise) is an exposition that the reality of victory is restricted to Him, the Exalted. Had victory been a matter of numbers, strength, and military might (الشوكة), the turn of fortune that day would have belonged to the polytheists due to their superior numbers and strength over the Muslims, who were few and weak.

He justified the entire content of the verse and the preceding verse with His saying: «*إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ*» (Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise). By His might (عزته), He granted them victory and aided them, and by His wisdom (حكمته), He fashioned His victory in this specific manner.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*إِذْ يُغَشِّيكُمُ النُّعاسَ أَمَنَةً مِنْهُ*» **When He covered you with a slumber as a security from Him** (8:11) to the end of the verse. **Slumber** (النعاس) is the beginning of sleep, which is light. **Covering** (التغشية) means surrounding. **Security** (الأمنة) is safety. His saying: «*أَمَنَةً*» (as a security) means from Allah; it is also said to mean from the enemy. **Defilement** (الرجز) is filth (الرجس) and impurity. What is meant by the "defilement of Satan" (رجز الشيطان) is the impurity that affects the heart from his whispering (وسوسته) and enticement (تسويله).

The meaning of the verse is: The victory and the aiding with good news and reassurance of hearts occurred at a time when slumber overtook you due to the security Allah poured into your hearts. Thus you slept; had you been fearful and terrified, neither slumber nor sleep would have overtaken you. He sent down rain upon you to purify you with it, to remove from you the whispering of Satan, to fortify your hearts and strengthen them—which is a metaphor for courage—and to make your feet firm through the rain during the battle, either by compacting the sand or by the steadfastness of your hearts.

The verse supports the reports that the polytheists reached the water before the Muslims. The Muslims camped on a sand dune, woke up in a state of ritual impurity (محدثين ومجنبين), and were afflicted by thirst. Satan whispered to them, saying: "Your enemy has reached the water before you, while you are praying in a state of major and minor ritual impurity, and your feet are sinking into the sand." Then Allah sent rain upon them until they performed the major ritual ablution (الغسل) from *janābah* and purified themselves from minor impurity. Their ground became firm, while the ground of their enemy became muddy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*إِذْ يُوحِي رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلائِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَأُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ*» **When your Lord revealed to the angels: ‘I am with you, so steady the faithful. I will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless’** (8:12) to the end of the verse. The status of the adverbial "when" (إذ) at the beginning of the verse is like its status in His saying: «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» (When you appealed to your Lord) and His saying: «*إِذْ يُغَشِّيكُمُ النُّعاسَ*» (When He covered you with a slumber). The meaning of the verse is clear.

As for His saying: «*فَاضْرِبُوا فَوْقَ الْأَعْناقِ وَاضْرِبُوا مِنْهُمْ كُلَّ بَنانٍ*» **so strike their necks and strike all their fingertips** (8:12), it appears that "above the necks" (فوق الأعناق) refers to the heads, and "every fingertip" (كل بنان) refers to all the extremities of the hands and feet, or the fingers of the hands so they would be unable to carry or grasp weapons.

It is possible that the command «*فَاضْرِبُوا*» (so strike) is addressed to the angels, as is the first impression that comes to mind. In that case, the meaning of striking above the necks and every fingertip is either literal or a metaphor for humiliating them and neutralizing the power of their hands through terror. It is also possible that the address is to the believers, intended to encourage them against their enemy by steadying their feet, fortifying their hearts, and inciting them against the polytheists.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَاقُّوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَمَنْ يُشاقِقِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَإِنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ*» **That is because they defied Allah and His Apostle; and whoever defies Allah and His Apostle, then Allah is indeed severe in retribution** (8:13). **Defiance** (المشاقة) is opposition. Its root is *shaqq* (شق), meaning a side or part, as if the opponent leans toward a side (شق) other than the side of the one he opposes. The meaning is that this punishment befell the polytheists because they opposed Allah and His Messenger and persisted in doing so; and whoever defies Allah and His Messenger, then Allah is indeed severe in retribution.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ذلِكُمْ فَذُوقُوهُ وَأَنَّ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابَ النَّارِ*» **‘That [is your punishment], so taste it,’ and [know] that for the faithless is the punishment of the Fire** (8:14). This is a stern address to the disbelievers, referring to the disgrace that has befallen them and commanding them to taste it, while reminding them that beyond this lies the punishment of the Fire.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*, Ibn Abbas said: "On the day of Badr, when the people lined up for battle, Abu Jahl said: 'O Allah, whichever of the two parties is more guided, grant it victory.' The Muslims appealed for help, so the angels descended and the verse was revealed: «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» (When you appealed to your Lord) to the end."

It is also said that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the large number of polytheists and the small number of Muslims, he faced the *Qiblah* and said: "O Allah, fulfill for me what You promised me. O Allah, if this group is destroyed, You will not be worshipped on earth." He continued to call upon his Lord with his hands outstretched until his cloak fell from his shoulders. Then Allah revealed: «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» (When you appealed to your Lord). This is narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab, al-Suddi, and Abu Salih, and it is what is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

He said: "When evening came and night covered the Messenger of Allah, Allah cast slumber upon his companions. They had camped in a place with much sand where feet could not remain firm. Then Allah sent down a light rain (رذاذاً) upon them until it compacted the ground and steadied their feet. The rain upon Quraysh was like a heavy downpour (العزالي). Allah cast terror into their hearts, as He, the Exalted, said: «*سَأُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ*» (I will cast terror into the hearts of the faithless)."

**I say:** The wording of the verse: «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» (When you appealed to your Lord) etc., does not align with it being revealed on the day of Badr immediately following their appeal. Rather, the context indicates it was revealed along with His saying: «*يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ*» (They ask you about the spoils) and the subsequent verses. These verses narrate a past event and express Allah’s favor upon the Muslims for the signs of victory and various blessings He bestowed upon them, so they might be grateful to Him and obey Him in what He commands and forbids.

Perhaps the mention of the verse being revealed after their appeal refers to the correspondence of the verse's content with the event, which is a frequent occurrence in narrations concerning the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahr Ashub: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said while in the arbor (العريش): "O Allah, if You destroy this group today, You will not be worshipped after this day." Then «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» was revealed. He came out saying: (**سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ**) — "The host shall be routed and they shall turn their backs." Allah aided him with five thousand marked angels, made the Muslims appear numerous in the eyes of the polytheists, and made the polytheists appear few in their eyes. Then it was revealed: (**وَهُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الْقُصْوى**) — "and they were at the further bank" of the valley behind the sandhill (العقنقل), while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was at the nearer bank (العدوة الدنيا) by the well.

**I say:** The commentary on this is the same as the previous one.

In *al-Majma‘*: Al-Balkhi mentioned from al-Hasan that the verse: «*وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللهُ*» (And when Allah promised you) was revealed before His saying: «*كَما أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَيْتِكَ بِالْحَقِ*» (Just as your Lord brought you out from your home with a true purpose), though in the recitation it comes after it.

**I say:** The fact that the event described in one verse preceded the event in another does not necessitate that it was revealed first. There is no evidence from the context to support what he mentioned.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Yahya al-Khath‘ami from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: «*وَإِذْ يَعِدُكُمُ اللهُ إِحْدَى الطَّائِفَتَيْنِ ـ أَنَّها لَكُمْ وَتَوَدُّونَ أَنَّ غَيْرَ ذاتِ الشَّوْكَةِ تَكُونُ لَكُمْ*» — he said: "The *shawkah* (sting/might) is that which involves fighting."

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated the same in his *Tafsir*.

In *al-Majma‘*, the historians (أصحاب السير) said—and Abu Hamzah and Ali ibn Ibrahim mentioned in their commentaries, their reports being merged: "Abu Sufyan approached with the caravan of Quraysh from Syria, containing their wealth—it was the *Latīmah* (perfume caravan)—with forty riders from Quraysh. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) urged his companions to go out to seize it, saying: 'Perhaps Allah will grant it to you as spoils.' The people responded; some were light-hearted and others heavy, not thinking that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would encounter a plot or war. They went out desiring only Abu Sufyan, and the riders saw it only as booty for themselves.

When Abu Sufyan heard of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) march, he hired Damdam ibn Amr al-Ghifari and sent him to Mecca, ordering him to reach Quraysh and call them to arms, informing them that Muhammad and his companions had intercepted their caravan. Damdam set out quickly for Mecca.

Three nights before Damdam’s arrival, Atikah bint Abd al-Muttalib had a dream in which a man approached on his camel shouting: 'O people of Ghalib! Hasten to your places of slaughter!' Then he stood with his camel atop Abu Qubays, took a stone, and rolled it down the mountain. There was not a single house among the houses of Quraysh that was not struck by a fragment of it. She woke up terrified and told al-Abbas, who told Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah. Utbah said: 'This is a calamity that will occur in Quraysh.' The dream spread among them and reached Abu Jahl, who said: 'This is a second prophetess among the Banu Abd al-Muttalib! By al-Lat and al-Uzza, we shall wait three days; if what she saw is true, so be it, otherwise we shall write a document between us stating that no household among the Arabs has men and women more lying than the Banu Hashim.'

On the third day, Damdam came to them shouting at the top of his voice: 'O people of Ghalib! O people of Ghalib! The *Latīmah*! The *Latīmah*! The caravan! The caravan! Catch up, though I do not think you will. Muhammad and the apostates (الصباة) from the people of Yathrib have come out to intercept your caravan.' They prepared to leave, and every great man of Quraysh contributed wealth to equip the army. They said: 'Whoever does not go out, we shall demolish his house.' Al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Nawfal ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and Aqil ibn Abi Talib went out with them. They also brought singing girls (القيان) playing drums.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out with three hundred and thirteen men. When he was near Badr, he captured a scout for the enemy who informed him about them. In the report of Abu Hamzah: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) also sent a scout of his own for the caravan named Adi. When he returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and told him where he had left the caravan, Gabriel descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of the departure of the polytheists from Mecca. He consulted his companions regarding seeking the caravan or fighting the army. Abu Bakr stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, it is Quraysh and their pride; they have never believed since they disbelieved, nor have they been humbled since they were mighty, and we have not come out prepared for war.' In Abu Hamzah’s report: 'I am knowledgeable about this road; Adi left the caravan at such-and-such place, they traveled and we traveled, so we and the people will be at the water of Badr on such-and-such day like two racing horses.' He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Sit down,' and he sat. Then Umar ibn al-Khattab stood up and said the same, and he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Sit down,' and he sat.

Then al-Miqdad stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, it is Quraysh and their pride, but we have believed in you, trusted you, and witnessed that what you have brought is the truth. By Allah, if you ordered us to wade through the embers of *Ghadā* and the thorns of *Harās*, we would wade through them with you. By Allah, we do not say to you as the Children of Israel said to Moses: "Go, you and your Lord, and fight; we will stay here sitting." Rather, we say: Go forth by the command of your Lord, for we are fighting alongside you.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prayed for his well-being for those words.

Then he said: 'Advise me, O people,' intending the Ansar because they were the majority and because when they pledged allegiance at al-Aqabah, they said: 'We are free from your protection until you reach our homes; then you are under our protection, and we shall defend you as we defend our children and women.' He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) feared that the Ansar might not see it as their duty to help him except against those who attacked him in Medina, and that they were not obliged to help him outside Medina.

Sa‘d ibn Mu‘adh stood up and said: 'May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, it seems you mean us.' He said: 'Yes.' Sa‘d said: 'May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, we have believed in you, trusted you, and witnessed that what you have brought is the truth from Allah. So command us as you wish, take from our wealth what you wish, and leave what you wish. By Allah, if you ordered us to wade through this sea, we would wade through it with you. Perhaps Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will show you from us that which will delight your eye. So lead us with the blessing of Allah.'

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rejoiced at this and said: 'Proceed with the blessing of Allah, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has promised me one of the two parties, and Allah never breaks His promise. By Allah, it is as if I am looking at the place of slaughter of Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah, Shaybah ibn Rabi‘ah, and so-and-so and so-and-so.' (1)

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered the departure and set out for Badr, which is a well. In the report of Abu Hamzah al-Thumali: Badr was a man from Juhaynah, and the water belonged to him, so the water was named after him. Quraysh approached and sent their slaves to fetch water. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) seized them and asked: 'Who are you?' They said: 'We are the slaves of Quraysh.' They asked: 'Where is the caravan?' They said: 'We have no knowledge of the caravan.' They began to beat them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was praying; he finished his prayer and said: 'If they tell you the truth, you beat them, and if they lie to you, you leave them.' They brought them to him, and he asked: 'Who are you?' They said: 'O Muhammad, we are the slaves of Quraysh.' He asked: 'How many are the people?' They said: 'We have no knowledge of their number.' He asked: 'How many camels do they slaughter each day?' They said: 'Nine to ten.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The people are nine hundred to a thousand men.' He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered them to be detained. News of this reached Quraysh, and they were terrified and regretted their march.

Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah met Abu al-Bakhtari ibn Hisham and said: 'Do you not see this transgression? By Allah, I cannot see where I am stepping. We came out to defend our caravan, and it has escaped, yet we have come out of transgression and enmity. By Allah, a transgressing people never prosper. I wish that the wealth of the Banu Abd Manaf in the caravan had gone and we had not made this march.' Abu al-Bakhtari said to him: 'You are a leader among the leaders of Quraysh; go among the people and take responsibility for the caravan that Muhammad and his companions intercepted at Nakhlah (2) and the blood of Ibn al-Hadrami, for he was your ally.'

He said: 'I take that upon myself, and no one among us will oppose except the son of al-Hanzaliyyah (meaning Abu Jahl). Go to him and inform him that I have taken responsibility for the caravan and the blood of Ibn al-Hadrami, who is my ally, and I shall pay his blood money.'

He said: 'I went to his tent and informed him of that. He said: "Utbah is being partisan toward Muhammad, for he is from the Banu Abd Manaf and his son is with him. He wants to discourage the people. No, by al-Lat and al-Uzza, not until we invade Yathrib or take them as prisoners and enter them into Mecca so the Arabs hear of it."' Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utbah was with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

When Abu Sufyan had passed with the caravan, he sent word to Quraysh: 'Allah has saved your caravan, so return and leave Muhammad to the Arabs; push him away as long as he can be pushed. If you do not return, then send back the singing girls.' The messenger reached them at al-Juhfah. Utbah wanted to return, but Abu Jahl and the Banu Makhzum refused, and they sent back the singing girls from al-Juhfah.

He said: The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were terrified when they heard of the large numbers of Quraysh. They appealed for help and supplicated, so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed: «*إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ*» (When you appealed to your Lord) and what follows.

Al-Tabarsi said: When morning came on the day of Badr, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arranged his companions. In his camp were two horses: a horse for al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and a horse for al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad. In his camp were seventy camels which they took turns riding. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام), and Marthad ibn Abi Marthad al-Ghanawi took turns on a camel belonging to Marthad. In the camp of Quraysh were four hundred horses, or it is said two hundred.

When Quraysh saw the small number of the Messenger of Allah’s (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) companions, Abu Jahl said: 'They are but a single meal (أكلة رأس); if we sent our slaves against them, they would seize them by hand.' Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah said: 'Do you see any ambush or reinforcements for them?' They sent Umayr ibn Wahb al-Jumahi, who was a brave horseman. He circled on his horse around the camp of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), then returned and said: 'They have no ambush or reinforcements, but the camels of Yathrib have carried certain death. Do you not see them silent, not speaking, licking their lips like snakes? They have no refuge but their swords. I do not see them turning back until they are killed, and they will not be killed until they kill an equal number of you. So consider your opinion.' Abu Jahl said to him: 'You lie and have turned coward.'

Then Allah revealed: «*وَإِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها*» **And if they incline toward peace, then you [too] incline toward it** (8:61). The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent word to them saying: 'O assembly of Quraysh, I dislike starting with you, so leave me to the Arabs and return.' Utbah said: 'No people ever rejected this and prospered.' Then he rode a red camel of his and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) looked at him as he moved between the two camps forbidding the fight. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'If there is good in anyone, it is with the owner of the red camel; if they obey him, they will be guided.'

Utbah gave a speech and said: 'O assembly of Quraysh, obey me today and disobey me forever. Muhammad has kin and protection, and he is your cousin. Leave him to the Arabs; if he is truthful, you will be the most honored through him, and if he is a liar, the wolves of the Arabs will suffice you against him.' This enraged Abu Jahl, who said to him: 'You have turned coward and your lungs have swollen with fear.' He replied: 'O you who yellows his posterior, does someone like me turn coward? Quraysh will soon know which of us is more ignoble and cowardly, and which of us is the corrupter of his people.'

He put on his armor and stepped forward with his brother Shaybah and his son al-Walid, saying: 'O Muhammad, send out to us our equals from Quraysh.' Three men from the Ansar came out and identified themselves. They said: 'Return; we only want our equals from Quraysh.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) looked at Ubaydah ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib—who was seventy years old that day—and said: 'Stand up, O Ubaydah.' He looked at Hamzah and said: 'Stand up, O uncle.' Then he looked at Ali ibn Abi Talib and said: 'Stand up, O Ali'—and he was the youngest of them—'and seek your right which Allah has appointed for you, for Quraysh has come with their pride and boastfulness, wanting to put out the light of Allah, but Allah refuses except to perfect His light.' Then he said: 'O Ubaydah, you take Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah.' He said to Hamzah: 'You take Shaybah.' And he said to Ali: 'You take al-Walid.'

They went until they reached the men, who said: 'Noble equals.' Ubaydah charged at Utbah and struck him on the head, splitting his skull. Utbah struck Ubaydah on his leg, severing it, and they both fell. Shaybah charged at Hamzah and they exchanged sword blows until their swords were notched. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), charged at al-Walid and struck him on the shoulder vein, the sword exiting from his armpit. Ali said: 'Al-Walid took his right hand with his left and struck my head with it; I thought the sky had fallen to the earth.'

Then Hamzah and Shaybah grappled. The Muslims said: 'O Ali, do you not see that the dog has overcome your uncle?' Ali (عليه السلام) charged at him and said: 'O uncle, lower your head.' Hamzah was taller than Shaybah, so Hamzah tucked his head into his chest, and Ali struck him, cutting him in half. Then he came to Utbah, who still had a spark of life, and finished him off.

In another narration, Hamzah came out against Utbah, Ubaydah against Shaybah, and Ali against al-Walid. Hamzah killed Utbah, Ubaydah killed Shaybah, and Ali (عليه السلام) killed al-Walid. Shaybah had struck Ubaydah’s leg and cut it, so Hamzah and Ali rescued him. Hamzah and Ali carried Ubaydah to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He wept and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, am I not a martyr?' He said: 'Yes, you are the first martyr from my household.'

Abu Jahl said to Quraysh: 'Do not hurry, and do not be reckless as the sons of Rabi‘ah were. Take the people of Yathrib and slaughter them, and take Quraysh and seize them so we may enter them into Mecca and make them realize the deviation they are upon.'

Iblis came in the form of Suraqah ibn Malik ibn Ju‘shum and said to them: 'I am your protector; give me your banner.' They gave him the banner of the left wing, which was with the Banu Abd al-Dar. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) looked at him and said to his companions: 'Lower your gaze and grit your teeth.' He raised his hand and said: 'O Allah, if this group is destroyed, You will not be worshipped.' Then a trance (الغشي) overcame him, and when it cleared, he was wiping the sweat from his face and said: 'This is Gabriel, he has come to you with a thousand angels in succession.'

In *al-Amali*, with its chain to Imam al-Rida from his forefathers (عليه السلام): The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) traveled to Badr in the month of Ramadan and conquered Mecca in the month of Ramadan.

**I say:** The historians and chroniclers are unanimous on this. Al-Ya‘qubi said in his *History*: "The Battle of Badr was on Friday, thirteen nights remaining of the month of Ramadan, eighteen months after his arrival in Medina."

Al-Waqidi said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) reached the valley of Badr on the eve of Friday, the seventeenth of Ramadan. He sent Ali, al-Zubayr, Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas, and Basbas ibn Amr to scout for water. They found the water-camels of Quraysh with their water-carriers. They captured them while some escaped. They brought them to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was standing in prayer. The Muslims questioned them and they said: 'We are the water-carriers of Quraysh; they sent us to fetch water for them.' They beat them. When they were beaten, they said: 'We belong to Abu Sufyan and we are with the caravan, and the caravan is at this sandhill.' Whenever they said that, they stopped beating them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) finished his prayer and said: 'If they tell you the truth, you beat them, and if they lie to you, you leave them.'

When morning came, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) straightened the ranks and addressed the Muslims. He praised Allah and lauded Him, then said:

'As for what follows: I urge you to that which Allah has urged you, and I forbid you from that which Allah has forbidden you. Indeed, Allah is great in His status; He commands the truth, loves sincerity, and gives to the people of good according to their stations with Him—by it they are remembered, and by it they excel. You have woken up in one of the stations of truth; Allah accepts nothing from anyone therein except what is intended for His sake. Indeed, patience in places of hardship is that by which Allah relieves anxiety and saves from grief; through it you attain salvation in the Hereafter. Among you is the Prophet of Allah, warning you and commanding you. So be ashamed today that Allah should see anything of your affairs for which He would loathe you, for He, the Exalted, says: (**لَمَقْتُ اللهِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ مَقْتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ**) — "Allah’s loathing is greater than your loathing of yourselves." Look into what He has commanded you in His Book, and shown you of His signs, and how He has honored you after humiliation. So be humble before Him that your Lord may be pleased with you, and show your Lord in these stations a matter by which you deserve what He promised you of His mercy and forgiveness. For His promise is true, His word is sincere, and His punishment is severe. I and you are only by Allah, the Living, the Sustainer; to Him we lean our backs, in Him we seek protection, upon Him we rely, and to Him is the return. May Allah forgive me and the Muslims.'

In *al-Majma‘*: A group of commentators like Ibn Abbas and others mentioned: Gabriel said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the day of Badr: 'Take a handful of dust and throw it at them.' When the two hosts met, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to Ali: 'Give me a handful of pebbles from the valley.' He handed him a palmful of pebbles with dust on them. He threw it into the faces of the people and said: 'May the faces be disgraced!' There was not a single polytheist but that some of it entered his eyes, mouth, and nostrils. Then the believers followed them, killing and capturing them. That throw was the cause of the enemy's defeat.

In *al-Amali*, with its chain to Ibn Abbas, he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood over the slain of Badr and said: 'May Allah reward you as an evil group; you called me a liar when I was truthful and betrayed me when I was trustworthy.' Then he turned to Abu Jahl ibn Hisham and said: 'This one was more defiant toward Allah than Pharaoh. When Pharaoh was certain of destruction, he declared the oneness of Allah, but when this one was certain of destruction, he called upon al-Lat and al-Uzza.'

In *al-Maghazi* by al-Waqidi: On the day of Badr, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) ordered the well to be filled. Then he ordered the slain to be thrown into it, all except Umayyah ibn Khalaf, for he was a fat man and had bloated that day. When they wanted to throw him, his flesh began to fall apart. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Leave him.' So they left him and threw enough dust and stones over him to cover him.

Then he stood over the people of the well and called them man by man: 'Have you found what your Lord promised you to be true? For I have found what my Lord promised me to be true. What an evil people you were to your Prophet; you called me a liar while the people believed me, you expelled me while the people sheltered me, and you fought me while the people helped me.' They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do you call upon a people who have died?' He said: 'They have known that what their Lord promised them is true.' In another narration: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'You do not hear what I say better than they do, but they cannot answer me.'

He said: The defeat of Quraysh was when the sun began to decline. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stayed at Badr and ordered Abdullah ibn Ka‘b to collect the spoils and carry them, and ordered a group of his companions to assist him. He prayed Asr at Badr and then departed. He passed by al-Uthayl before sunset, camped there, and spent the night. His companions had wounds, but they were not many. He ordered Dhakwan ibn Abd Qays to guard the Muslims until the end of the night, then he departed.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in a long report: Abu Jahl came out from between the ranks and said: 'O Allah, Muhammad has severed our kinship and brought us what we do not recognize, so destroy him this morning.' Then Allah revealed to His Messenger: «*إِنْ تَسْتَفْتِحُوا فَقَدْ جاءَكُمُ الْفَتْحُ ـ وَإِنْ تَنْتَهُوا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ ـ وَإِنْ تَعُودُوا نَعُدْ وَلَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْكُمْ فِئَتُكُمْ شَيْئاً ـ وَلَوْ كَثُرَتْ وَأَنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ*» **If you seek a verdict, the verdict has certainly come to you; and if you desist, it will be better for you; but if you revert, We [too] shall revert, and your host will not avail you anything, though it be numerous, and [know] that Allah is with the faithful** (8:19).

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) took a palmful of pebbles and threw it into the faces of Quraysh and said: 'May the faces be disgraced!' Allah sent winds that struck the faces of Quraysh, and the defeat occurred. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'O Allah, let not the Pharaoh of this nation, Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, escape.' Seventy of them were killed and seventy were captured.

Amr ibn al-Jamuh met Abu Jahl; Amr struck Abu Jahl on his thigh, and Abu Jahl struck Amr on his hand, severing it from the arm so it hung by the skin. Amr stepped on his hand with his foot and pulled upward until the skin tore and he threw his hand away.

Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud said: I reached Abu Jahl while he was wallowing in his blood. I said: 'Praise be to Allah who has disgraced you.' He raised his head and said: 'Allah only disgraces a slave. O son of the mother of a slave, for whom is the victory, woe to you?' I said: 'For Allah and His Messenger, and I am going to kill you.' I placed my foot on his neck. He said: 'You have climbed a difficult ascent, O little shepherd of sheep. Know that nothing is harder for me than your killing me today. Would that my killing had been handled by a man from the Muttalibis or a man from the allies (الأحلاف).' I removed a helmet that was on his head, killed him, took his head, and brought it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, good news! This is the head of Abu Jahl ibn Hisham.' He prostrated to Allah in gratitude.

In *al-Irshad* by al-Mufid: Then the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) challenged al-As ibn Sa‘id ibn al-As after others had shrunk back from him; it was not long before he killed him. Hanzalah ibn Abi Sufyan came out against him and he killed him. After him, Tu‘aymah ibn Adi came out and he killed him. Then he killed Nawfal ibn Khuwaylid, who was one of the devils of Quraysh. He continued killing them one after another until he had accounted for half of their slain, who were seventy men. All the Muslims present at Badr, along with three thousand marked angels, accounted for one half, and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) alone accounted for the other half.

In *al-Irshad* also: The narrators of both the general public (العامة) and the elite (الخاصة) have recorded the names of those whom the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) personally killed at Badr from among the polytheists, with agreement and consensus in what they transmitted. Among those they named: al-Walid ibn Utbah as we mentioned—he was brave, daring, insolent, and lethal, feared by men; al-As ibn Sa‘id, who was a great terror feared by heroes, and he was the one from whom Umar ibn al-Khattab turned away—his story is famous and we shall explain it; Tu‘aymah ibn Adi ibn Nawfal—he was one of the heads of misguidance; and Nawfal ibn Khuwaylid, who was among the most hostile polytheists toward the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Quraysh used to give him precedence, honor him, and obey him. He was the one who bound Abu Bakr and Talhah together before the Hijrah in Mecca, tying them with a rope and torturing them from morning until night until he was petitioned regarding them. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) learned of his presence at Badr, he asked Allah to suffice him against him, saying: 'O Allah, suffice me against Nawfal ibn Khuwaylid.' So the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) killed him.

And Zam‘ah ibn al-Aswad (1), al-Harith ibn Zam‘ah, al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Dar, Umayr ibn Uthman ibn Ka‘b ibn Taym (the uncle of Talhah ibn Ubaydullah), Uthman and Malik the sons of Ubaydullah (the brothers of Talhah ibn Ubaydullah), Mas‘ud ibn Abi Umayyah ibn al-Mughirah, Qays ibn al-Fakih ibn al-Mughirah, Hudhayfah ibn Abi Hudhayfah ibn al-Mughirah, [Abu] Qays (1) ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Hanzalah ibn Abi Sufyan, Amr ibn Makhzum, Abu Mundhir ibn Abi Rifa‘ah, Munabbih ibn al-Hajjaj al-Sahmi, al-As ibn Munabbih, Alqamah ibn Kaldah, Abu al-As ibn Qays ibn Adi, Mu‘awiyah ibn al-Mughirah ibn Abi al-As, Lawdhan ibn Rabi‘ah, Abdullah ibn al-Mundhir ibn Abi Rifa‘ah, Mas‘ud ibn Umayyah ibn al-Mughirah, Hajib ibn al-Sa’ib ibn Uwaymir, Aws ibn al-Mughirah ibn Lawdhan, Zayd ibn Malis, Asim ibn Abi Awf, Sa‘id ibn Wahb (ally of Banu Amir), Mu‘awiyah ibn [Amr ibn] Abd al-Qays, Abdullah ibn Jamil ibn Zuhayr ibn al-Harith ibn Asad, al-Sa’ib ibn Malik, Abu al-Hakam ibn al-Akhnas, and Hisham ibn Abi Umayyah ibn al-Mughirah.

That makes thirty-five men—excluding those about whom there is disagreement or in whose killing others shared with the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام). They are more than half of those killed at Badr, as we previously stated.

**I say:** Others mentioned, as in *al-Majma‘*, that he killed twenty-seven men on the day of Badr. Al-Waqidi mentioned that the number agreed upon by the transmitters and narrators of those he killed is nine men, while the rest are disputed.

However, deep research into the story and the surrounding poetry and the various events that occurred afterward makes one suspicious of this disagreement. It has been narrated from Muhammad ibn Ishaq that most of the polytheists killed on the day of Badr were slain by Ali (عليه السلام).

Al-Waqidi, in what Ibn Abi al-Hadid cited, counted fifty-two men among the polytheists killed in the Battle of Badr and attributed the killing of twenty-four of them to him (عليه السلام), whether he killed them alone or shared with others.

From the poetry of Usayd ibn Abi Iyas inciting the polytheists of Quraysh against Ali (عليه السلام), as found in *al-Irshad* and *al-Manaqib*:

*In every gathering, a goal has disgraced you,*
*A grief that surpasses the swiftest of horses.*
*By Allah, do you not recognize [the shame]?*
*The noble free man may recognize and feel shame.*
*This is the son of Fatimah who has annihilated you,*
*By slaughter and killing that was swift and decisive.*
*Give him tribute and beware of his strike,*
*The act of the lowly and his trade did not profit.*
*Where are the elders and where is every pillar*
*In calamities, and where is the beauty of the valley?*
*He annihilated them by swift death and strikes he invents,*
*With the sword he works its edge, never turning it aside.*

In *al-Irshad*, Shu‘bah narrated from Abu Ishaq from Harith ibn Mudarrab, who said: I heard Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) say: "We were present at Badr, and there was no horseman among us except al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad. I saw us on the night of Badr, and there was none among us but that he slept, except the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); he was standing at the base of a tree, praying and supplicating until morning."

**I say:** The narrations regarding the story of Badr are very numerous. We have limited ourselves to what clarifies the understanding of the verses' contents. Some reports will follow, Allah willing, in the course of discussing the subsequent verses referring to certain aspects of the story.

## (Index of the Names of the Martyrs of Badr, may Allah be pleased with them)

In *al-Bihar*, from al-Waqidi, who said: Abdullah ibn Ja‘far told me: I asked al-Zuhri: "How many Muslims were martyred at Badr?" He said: "Fourteen: six from the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and eight from the Helpers (الأنصار)."

He said: From the Banu al-Muttalib ibn Abd Manaf: Ubaydah ibn al-Harith, killed by Utbah—and in a narration other than al-Waqidi's, killed by Shaybah—the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) buried him at al-Safra. From the Banu Zuhrah: Umayr ibn Abi Waqqas—killed by Amr ibn Abd Wudd, the horseman of the Confederates (الأحزاب). And Umayr ibn Abd Wudd, Dhu al-Shimalayn, an ally of the Banu Zuhrah—killed by Abu Usamah al-Jushami. From the Banu Adi: Aqil ibn Abi al-Bukayr—an ally of theirs from the Banu Sa‘d, killed by Malik ibn Zuhayr. And Mihja‘, the freedman of Umar ibn al-Khattab, killed by Amir ibn al-Hadrami—it is said that Mihja‘ was the first of the Emigrants to be killed. From the Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr: Safwan ibn Bayda’—killed by Tu‘aymah ibn Adi.

From the Helpers (الأنصار), then from the Banu Amr ibn Awf: Mubashir ibn Abd al-Mundhir, killed by Abu Thawr. And Sa‘d ibn Khaythamah, killed by Amr ibn Abd Wudd—it is also said Tu‘aymah ibn Adi. From the Banu Adi ibn al-Najjar: Harithah ibn Suraqah—Hannan ibn al-Arqah shot him with an arrow that hit his throat and killed him. From the Banu Malik ibn al-Najjar: Awf and Mu‘awwidh, the sons of Afra’, both killed by Abu Jahl. From the Banu Salamah: Umayr ibn al-Humam ibn al-Jamuh, killed by Khalid ibn al-A‘lam—it is said he was the first of the Helpers to be killed, though it is also narrated that the first was Harithah ibn Suraqah. From the Banu Zurayq: Rafi‘ ibn al-Mu‘alla—killed by Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl. From the Banu al-Harith ibn al-Khazraj: Yazid ibn al-Harith—killed by Nawfal ibn Mu‘awiyah. These are the eight from the Helpers.

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that Anasah, the freedman of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), was killed at Badr. It is also narrated that Mu‘adh ibn Ma‘is was wounded at Badr and died from his wounds in Medina, and the son of Ubayd ibn al-Sakan was wounded and died from his wound.
